title: Revelation 14
intro: Rev 14 is the **three-angels'-messages chapter** — the doctrinal heart of historicist Adventism — and also the chapter that frames the **144,000** (vv. 1-5) and the **harvest / vintage** climax (vv. 14-20). Smith and Bohr converge on the **historicist spine**: Mt Zion = the redeemed; the three angels = three successive end-time messages preached from the early 19th century onward (first angel = hour-of-judgment 1840-1844; second angel = 1844 Babylon-fallen plus future Loud-Cry repeat; third angel = mark-of-the-beast warning culminating in Sabbath/Sunday issue); v. 13 special blessing on the dead in Christ from 1844; v. 14 Son-of-man = Christ at the second advent; vv. 14-16 harvest = ingathering of the righteous; vv. 17-20 vintage / winepress = the wicked. The divergences are concentrated in **three places**: (1) the **chronological starting point** of the first-angel proclamation, where Smith locks it to **1840-1844** Millerite preaching and Bohr extends it back to **1798** (the Rev 10:11 "prophesy again" / book unsealed); (2) the **scope** of the second angel, where Smith reads v. 8 primarily as the **1844 Millerite cry** and Bohr stages it as a **two-stage** message — 1844 + future Loud Cry of Rev 18:1-5; (3) the **chronology of the winepress** (vv. 17-20), where Smith reads the trampling as **post-millennial** (the executive judgment after the 1000 years, executed at the descent of the New Jerusalem) while Bohr reads it as **pre-second-coming / Armageddon-seventh-plague** (the wicked destroyed at Christ's return, with Rev 19:11ff as the parallel). The 144,000 framework Bohr adds beyond Smith is the **four-portraits** schema (Rev 7:1-8 sealing; 14:1-5 character; 15:2-4 victory; 19:1-9 jubilatory song), with v. 1 staged as **spiritual Mt Zion** (the worldwide remnant inside the city) over against the wicked grapes outside. Sources: Bohr, _From the Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 7-14, 66-70, 194, 221-227, 372-378; _Revelation's Seven Trumpets_, pp. 20-21, 147, 228, 246, 263, 357-358; _Revelation's Seven Seals_, pp. 282-283, 287; _Studies on Daniel 11_, on Rev 14:6, 7 and 9-11.

verses.text: And I looked, and, lo, a Lamb stood on the mount Sion, and with him an hundred forty and four thousand, having his Father's name written in their foreheads.

pioneerReadings.html: EGW reads Mt Zion as the **heavenly Mt Zion** where the redeemed gather. In her first-vision narrative she records Christ's words at the temple: "_Only the 144,000 enter this place,_" and we shouted, 'Alleluia'" (EW 18.2). In GC the same scene is unpacked: the 144,000 "stand upon Mount Zion with the Lamb, 'having the harps of God'" (GC 648-649). The **Father's name in the foreheads** = **the seal of God**, which she identifies elsewhere as the Sabbath (EW 254-258, 7BC 970). Christ "formed within" gives them the character-likeness required for translation (7BC 978.2: "_Christ Formed Within_—[Revelation 14:1-3 quoted.]").

Smith identifies Mt Zion as **heavenly** (the seat of the New Jerusalem above, not earthly Palestine). On the 144,000: "**This view of the 144,000 is given to us out of place chronologically. They are the same as those brought to view in chapter 7:1-8, who are sealed during the sounding of the angel ascending from the east. After their sealing, they pass through the time of trouble such as never was (Daniel 12:1) and are finally delivered, being translated to heaven without seeing death, at the second coming of Christ**" (DAR 582.4-583.1). On the name in the foreheads: "**'Having his Father's name written in their foreheads.' This expression must denote the perfection of character which they have attained, in being made ready for translation**" (DAR 583.2).

James White: Mt Zion = the **heavenly Mt Zion where the sanctuary stands** — "_Thou shalt bring them in, and plant them in the mountain of thine inheritance, in the place, O Lord, which thou hast made for thee to dwell in; in the Sanctuary, O Lord, which thy hands have established_" (Exod 15:17, opening of SATDSD 2.1). The 144,000's standing on Mt Zion is the **consummation** of the sanctuary movement Christ began Oct 22, 1844 — when the saints finally gather to the heavenly Mt Zion at translation.

Andrews places the entire Rev 14:1-5 vision in **the future** at the second advent — but ties it inseparably to the contemporary preaching of the three messages (vv. 6-12). "It was but a few years ago that all advent believers were united in applying this prophecy to the present generation" (TMR 10.1). The 144,000 standing with the Lamb on Mt Zion is the **end-state portrait** of the same company now keeping the commandments under the third angel; the vision is set out of chronological order so John can see both the **destination** (vv. 1-5) and the **journey** (vv. 6-12) in one block. Mt Zion = **heavenly Mt Zion**, not earthly (TMR 11.3 — the saints "shall all be caught up to meet the Lord in the air," then stand on the mount). **Convergent with Smith** on Mt Zion as heavenly.

Haskell stages Rev 14:1 as the **post-conflict victory portrait** of the 144,000: "The Lamb stood on Mount Zion, where the city of the living God stands. There in the heavenly" assembly is the Lamb as Conqueror, no longer slain, with the sealed company gathered (SSP 246.1). He explicitly cross-links to Rev 7: "In the year 1848, the four angels of the seventh chapter of Revelation took their stand upon the four corners of the earth, to hold the winds of strife until the servants of God were sealed. 'And there were sealed an hundred and forty and four thousand'" (SSP 247.1) — putting the **sealing of the 144,000 as commencing in 1848** (a sharper date-stamp than Smith). Mt Zion = **heavenly Mt Zion** (convergent with Smith).

Loughborough as movement historian frames the 144,000 on Mt Zion as the **end-state of the same advent body** that emerged from the Disappointment — "_the time proclamation was right. By the most careful review of their reckoning of the 2300 days, they could find no mistake_" (GSAM 195.3). The 144,000 are the Sabbath-keeping remnant gathered after 1844 and finally translated. Convergent with Smith, Andrews, Haskell.

modernReadings.citation: _From the Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 7-14, 194
modernReadings.html: Mt Zion is the worldwide spiritual gathering of the remnant. Verbatim: "**God's people are depicted as the harvest of the earth inside the city and the wicked are portrayed as the grapes gathered outside the city... Mount Zion here is the worldwide spiritual gathering of God's remnant people**" (p. 194). Bohr stages Rev 14:1-5 as one of **four portraits** of the 144,000: "**Revelation 14:1-5: Emphasis on the character of the 144,000**" (_Revelation's Seven Seals_, pp. 282-283). On the Father's name: Bohr cites EGW (GC 648-649) verbatim, which intermingles Rev 14:1-5 line by line: "_Upon Mount Zion stood the Lamb, and with Him an hundred forty and four thousand, having His Father's name written on their foreheads (Revelation 14:1)_" (_Close of Probation_, p. 12). **Convergent with extension**: both readings agree the 144,000 are the last-generation translated saints; Bohr adds the explicit **four-portraits literary framework** and the **spiritual-Mt-Zion-as-worldwide-gathering** reading where Smith leaves Mt Zion as the heavenly New Jerusalem locale.

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Convergent on substance; Bohr's four-portraits framework is a Rule-V harmonization (Rev 7 + 14 + 15 + 19 read together as one composite).

symbols.label: Symbols redefined
symbols.html: Mt Zion (Smith: heavenly New Jerusalem above; Bohr: worldwide spiritual gathering of remnant on earth, inside the spiritual city).

verses.text: And I heard a voice from heaven, as the voice of many waters, and as the voice of a great thunder: and I heard the voice of harpers harping with their harps:

pioneerReadings.html: EGW reads the voice from heaven as the 144,000's triumphant song before the throne, harping with the harps of God: "_Upon Mount Zion stood the Lamb, and with Him an hundred forty and four thousand, having His Father's name written on their foreheads... and I heard the voice of harpers harping with their harps_" (GC 648-649, Rev 14:1-3 quoted verbatim). The song is **the song of Moses and the Lamb** of Rev 15:3, which she ties directly to the deliverance experience of the last generation.

"**The voice that the prophet hears is the voice of the 144,000 themselves... they sing the song of Moses and the Lamb, a song which no man could learn but those who, having gained the victory over the beast, his image, and his mark, stand on the sea of glass, having the harps of God**" (DAR 583.3, cross-linking Rev 15:2-3).

Andrews treats vv. 1-5 as the end-state portrait of the same company addressed by vv. 6-12; the voice is the **victory song of the redeemed 144,000** sounded after the conflict with the beast is past. Convergent with Smith on the company singing rather than angels.

Haskell ties the voice to the **post-1844 sealed company** singing their unique song of deliverance, the same as the song of Moses and the Lamb (Rev 15:2-4). The harps are received only after passing through the time of trouble (cross-link to Rev 15:2).

modernReadings.citation: _From the Close of Probation to the New Earth_, p. 12, quoting GC 648-649 verbatim
modernReadings.html: "_And I heard a voice from heaven, as the voice of many waters, and as the voice of a great thunder: and I heard the voice of harpers harping with their harps (Revelation 14:2)_" (p. 12). Bohr cross-links this to the **Rev 15:2-4 victory-song portrait** of the 144,000.

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Convergent.

verses.text: And they sung as it were a new song before the throne, and before the four beasts, and the elders: and no man could learn that song but the hundred and forty and four thousand, which were redeemed from the earth.

pioneerReadings.html: EGW: the song is **unique to the 144,000's experience** — they alone learn it because they alone passed through the time of trouble such as never was and were translated without seeing death: "They are 'they which come out of great tribulation;' they have passed through the time of trouble such as never was since there was a nation" (GC 649.1, with Rev 14:3 quoted verbatim). Convergent with Smith: the song is one of **distinct end-time experience**.

Smith reads the song as the unique experience-song of the last generation: "**this song is one of experience, and it is the peculiar experience of the 144,000 which no other company can ever sing. They are the ones who are alive when the seven last plagues are poured out... and yet are sustained through this awful period, are at last delivered by the voice of God, and changed to immortality at the coming of Christ. This is an experience distinct from that of all others**" (DAR 583.3-584.1).

Andrews uses v. 3 as proof against the futurist-only reading: the 144,000 are explicitly "redeemed from the earth" — implying a real historical company that lived under the third angel and was finally redeemed. The vision is the **end-state** of those addressed by vv. 6-12 (TMR 11.1-13.2).

Haskell: "These men were redeemed from the earth, from among men,—the first fruits unto God and the Lamb. They have been snatched as brands from the burning" (SSP 249.1). The new song requires a unique experience — that of being the **living last-generation** delivered through translation.

modernReadings.citation: _From the Close of Probation to the New Earth_, p. 12, quoting GC 649 verbatim
modernReadings.html: "_And they sung as it were a new song before the throne... and no man could learn that song but the hundred and forty and four thousand which were redeemed from the earth. They are 'they which come out of great tribulation;' they have passed through the time of trouble such as never was since there was a nation_" (p. 12). Bohr links this to the **third 144,000-portrait** (Rev 15:2-4): "**Revelation 15:2-4: They sing the song of Moses and of the Lamb**" (_Revelation's Seven Seals_, p. 283).

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Convergent.

verses.text: These are they which were not defiled with women; for they are virgins. These are they which follow the Lamb whithersoever he goeth. These were redeemed from among men, being the firstfruits unto God and to the Lamb.

pioneerReadings.html: EGW reads "not defiled with women, for they are virgins" as **doctrinal purity** — the 144,000 have not joined the apostate churches (= the harlot Babylon and her daughters of Rev 17). "Walking as Christ Walked—Those who walk even as Christ walked, who are patient, gentle, kind, meek and lowly in heart... these will enter into the joy of their Lord" (7BC 949.2). The **firstfruits** are the special harvest of last-generation living saints translated without seeing death — distinct from the general resurrection harvest.

Smith reads **"women"** as a Rule-V symbol — corrupt churches: "**Women here, as in other places, where used as symbols, denote churches; and as those with whom these are not defiled are described as virgins, the women from whom they have kept themselves free, must denote corrupt churches; that is, Babylon, the mother, and her offspring, the apostate churches of the present day**" (DAR 584.2). On firstfruits: "**As the firstfruits of the harvest were the first ripe grain... so the 144,000 are the firstfruits of the great spiritual harvest of the redeemed to be gathered from the earth**" (DAR 585.1).

Andrews reads "these are they which follow the Lamb whithersoever he goeth" as the **distinctive obedience of the third-angel company**: those who keep all the commandments of God, including the Sabbath, even when other professed Christians refuse. The virginity figure parallels 2 Cor 11:2 (the church as pure bride) — Babylon's churches (vv. 8) are by contrast the defiled women.

Haskell: virginity = **doctrinal undefilement** — the 144,000 have not joined the fallen churches. The figure draws on Isa 4:1: "_seven women shall take hold of one man_" — the apostate churches forsake the true gospel and demand only the name. The firstfruits are the **last-generation translated saints**, distinct from the general harvest (vv. 14-16).

modernReadings.citation: _From the Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 12-13, 372-378, quoting GC 649
modernReadings.html: "_These are they which were not defiled with women; for they are virgins. These are they which follow the Lamb whithersoever He goeth (Revelation 14:4)_" (p. 12). Bohr explicitly reads the women symbol as corrupt churches and grounds the character-portrait of Rev 14:1-5 as the third-angel-message ripened character: "**the third angel's message contains the message of righteousness by faith but rather it is righteousness by faith**" (p. 374, on Rev 14:9-12 producing the 14:1-5 character). On firstfruits Bohr leaves the substance to the harvest-of-the-earth reading at vv. 14-16.

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Convergent. Both Smith and Bohr read women = corrupt churches via Rule V (Rev 17 self-defines the Babylon harlot).

symbols.label: Symbols redefined
symbols.html: Women (both: corrupt churches, primarily Babylon and her daughters per Rev 17).

verses.text: And in their mouth was found no guile: for they are without fault before the throne of God.

pioneerReadings.html: EGW reads "in their mouth no guile, without fault before the throne" as the **last-generation character of Christ-formed truth-tellers**: "None can behold with rapture the glory of God in His holy temple, who have neglected to acquire those characteristics that fit them for the chambers of fellowship with the King" (cross-ref). The 144,000 alone stand sinless before the throne having reflected Christ's character perfectly under the latter rain.

"**These constitute the company who go through the time of trouble such as never was, and are translated to heaven without seeing death... 'In their mouth was found no guile.' They are without fault before the throne of God. The mark of perfection is upon them**" (DAR 585.2).

Andrews links the faultlessness to the patience of the saints clause of v. 12: the same company that **keeps the commandments of God and the faith of Jesus** under the third angel is the one that stands without guile before the throne. Faultlessness is **achievement under the latter rain**, the climactic preparation of the remnant.

Haskell: "In their mouth was found no guile; for the soul temple had been so thoroughly cleansed before leaving earth that the human mouth became a channel for the words of God. When the mind of Christ takes full possession of a man, he thinks and speaks and acts as Christ Himself would act" (SSP 250.1). The faultlessness is **achieved character** wrought by the latter rain, not legal fiction.

modernReadings.citation: _From the Close of Probation to the New Earth_, p. 12, quoting GC 649 verbatim
modernReadings.html: "_And in their mouth was found no guile: for they are without fault before the throne of God (Revelation 14:5)_" (p. 12). Bohr ties this to the perfected character produced by the third angel's message: see p. 374 ("**the third angel's message... is righteousness by faith**").

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Convergent.

verses.text: And I saw another angel fly in the midst of heaven, having the everlasting gospel to preach unto them that dwell on the earth, and to every nation, and kindred, and tongue, and people,

pioneerReadings.html: EGW identifies the first angel as the **1840-1844 Millerite proclamation** of Christ's imminent return and the judgment hour: "This warning is brought to view in Revelation 14. Here is a threefold message represented as proclaimed by heavenly beings and immediately followed by the coming of the Son of man to reap 'the harvest of the earth.' The first of these warnings announces the approaching judgment" (GC 311.3). "This message is declared to be a part of 'the everlasting gospel.' The work of preaching the gospel has not been committed to angels, but has been entrusted to men. Holy angels have been employed in directing this work... but the actual proclamation of the gospel is performed by the servants of Christ upon the earth" (GC 312.1). The **everlasting gospel** = the same saving truth Paul preached, but now with the **judgment-hour additional element** distinctive to the last days. "Faithful men, who were obedient to the promptings of God's Spirit and the teachings of His word, were to proclaim this warning to the world" (GC 312.2).

Smith identifies the angel as a symbol of a **religious movement** preaching the everlasting gospel just prior to the Second Advent. The movement is the Millerite advent awakening of **1840-1844**: "**An angel is a messenger; and the symbol here used must denote a religious movement, having for its purpose the proclamation of a message to the world. The everlasting gospel is to be preached to every nation... This must be the great Advent movement of 1840-1844, when the message of the hour of judgment was proclaimed in this and other lands**" (DAR 585.3, condensed; the full historical documentation runs through pp. 585-588 with parallel European movements — Wolff, Bengel, Gaussen, Mason, etc.).

Miller identifies the first angel as the **immediate-second-coming proclamation** based on the 2300-day prophecy of Dan 8:14: "I found it plainly taught in the Scriptures that Jesus Christ will again descend to this earth, coming in the clouds of heaven, in all the glory of his Father" (Miller, in SLWM 51.3). The everlasting gospel for Miller = the gospel of the *coming kingdom*, distinct from any merely spiritual reign — preached now as imminent. Miller's calculation (1843/44) is the warrant for the loud-voice proclamation.

Litch reads the first angel as the **definite end-time judgment-hour cry** beginning with Miller's 1831 ministry and consummating in 1840-1844, with the 1840 fulfillment of his Ottoman-trumpet prophecy as the providential vindication. The everlasting gospel is the same gospel Paul preached, with the distinctive added clause "_the hour of His judgment is come_" that could only be true after the 2300-day terminus.

James White (in his biography of Miller and of Bates) treats the first angel as the **same movement that he and his fellow Adventists preached from 1840 onward**: the everlasting gospel of the coming kingdom, with the new chronological element of the judgment-hour now arrived. He documents Miller's nine-year solitary preaching, then the 1840 Boston conference where Litch's Ottoman prediction (Aug 11, 1840 fulfillment) confirmed the prophetic interpretation and ignited the wider movement (SLWM 98ff, LELJB 268.3).

Andrews offers the **canonical SDA exposition**: the first angel symbolizes "a body of men proclaiming" the everlasting gospel + the hour-of-judgment cry — the **Millerite Advent movement of 1831-1844** (TMR 24.2). He gives four reasons why the proclamation cannot be past, future, or applied to any age but the present: (1) no past age has ever made this proclamation (TMR 14.3); (2) had it been made earlier it would have been false because the prophecies fixing the judgment-time "_were closed up and sealed till the time of the end_" (TMR 16.2, Dan 8:17, 26; 12:4, 9); (3) the apostles plainly located the judgment in the future after the 1260 years (TMR 17-18); (4) the parable of the marriage supper (Luke 14:16-24) maps three calls to three angels (TMR 22.1). The angel "is the same as the one brought to view in Revelation 10" (TMR 26.1) — the swearing angel whose oath of "time no longer" terminates with the seventh-trumpet probationary close. **Convergent with Smith; PRIMARY treatment**.

Crosier's 1846 sanctuary article does not exposit Rev 14:6 directly, but supplies the **doctrinal substructure** for the first angel's hour-of-judgment cry: at the end of the 2300 days (Oct 22, 1844) Christ moved from the Holy to the Most Holy Place to begin **the investigative phase of the atonement** (SANC 19.6). Without Crosier's sanctuary correction the disappointment of 1844 would have invalidated the first-angel proclamation; with it, the first angel's "_hour of His judgment is come_" is confirmed — the judgment began Oct 22, 1844 in heaven, not on earth.

Haskell treats the first angel as the **judgment-hour Millerite cry of 1840-1844**, with the explicit refusal to date the message earlier: "After the strife and the turmoil of the great controversy... John had his attention called to scenes where the conflict was all ended" (SSP 246.1). The 144,000 (vv. 1-5) is the **destination**; the three angels (vv. 6-12) are the **journey**. The everlasting gospel + judgment-hour element together constitute a **new message** that could not have been preached in any past age.

Jones positions the first angel as the **demanded revival of true Protestantism against the Image of the Beast** — the same protest the Reformers made, now intensified for the final crisis: "the apostasy of Protestantism having developed the very image of that which was developed from the apostasy of Catholicism, there is demanded a revival of true Protestantism to protest against this apostate Protestantism, this Image of the Beast" (ECE 871.13). The everlasting gospel = the gospel of **worshiping the Creator** identified by the sign He himself set (the Sabbath), in opposition to all human authority in religion. The first angel + the three messages form a single counter-witness against the church-state union of vv. 9-11.

pioneerReadings.citation: CHR (Christ and His Righteousness) ch. on judgment-hour gospel
pioneerReadings.html: Waggoner emphasizes that the first angel's message is the **everlasting gospel** — emphasis on *gospel*. The judgment-hour element does not displace the saving gospel of righteousness by faith but provides the urgency for its proclamation. The gospel preached for fear-of-judgment alone is no gospel; the everlasting gospel is the same gospel Paul preached, now with the **distinctive end-time chronological clause**. Diverges modestly from Andrews' more chronology-forward framing: Waggoner stresses the **gospel content** as primary.

Bates' Rev 14 framework treats the first angel as the **1844 movement** that he himself participated in: "_Bring to remembrance things in 1844. Comparisons for that time are beyond our reach - past, present and future. The rushing from the sectional, sectarian branches of the nominal church (or Sardis state,) at that time, has no parallel_" (BP3 138.1). The everlasting gospel is the **Advent gospel** that drove believers out of the fallen Sardis-state churches in preparation for the seventh-day Sabbath restoration under the third angel.

Loughborough as movement historian places the first angel's proclamation precisely between **1831 (Miller's first preaching) and Oct 22, 1844 (Disappointment)**. After the Disappointment the same body of believers (Sabbath-keeping Adventists) carried forward the same message in its **enlarged threefold form** — "_the time proclamation was right. By the most careful review of their reckoning of the 2300 days, they could find no mistake, neither yet can any be found_" (GSAM 195.3). The everlasting gospel is the same gospel Paul preached, with the **chronological additional clause** that distinguishes the last-days proclamation from all prior preaching.

Fitch is one of the **first preachers** to embrace the Millerite first-angel proclamation (1841), and combined the everlasting-gospel cry with the imminent literal return: "_the two resurrections... will take place literally, as there laid down_" (LJL 14.2). Fitch died in October 1844 — he is foundational to the first-angel cry but his most famous contribution (the 1843 'Come Out of Her, My People' sermon) belongs to the second angel (v. 8).

modernReadings.citation: _From the Close of Probation to the New Earth_, p. 66; _Revelation's Seven Trumpets_, pp. 147, 228, 246, 263
modernReadings.html: Bohr extends the first-angel proclamation back to **1798** (the unsealing of Daniel under Rev 10) and frames the three angels as sent by the **triune Godhead**: "**In Revelation 14:6-13, the fourth and fifth trumpets have faded from view... Revelation 14:6-13 takes us back to the time when the remnant began to proclaim first angel's message to the world (beginning in 1798)**" (_Seven Trumpets_, p. 147). On the global scope: "**The Angel presents a message from the book that is global in extension—to every nation, kindred, tongue and people (Revelation 14:6)**" (_Seven Trumpets_, p. 246). On the Rev 10 / Rev 14 link: Bohr identifies **three common denominators** between Rev 10 and Rev 14:6-7 — global message, Creator and Sabbath, judgment hour (_Seven Trumpets_, p. 263). Bohr quotes EGW (GC 356): "_this message [Revelation 14:6, 7] is a part of the gospel which could be proclaimed only in the last days, for only then would it be true that the hour of judgment had come_" (_Seven Trumpets_, p. 228). **Diverges from Smith on chronological starting point**: Smith locks the angel to 1840-1844 (Millerite phase only); Bohr opens the window at 1798 (book-unsealed phase) and continues through 1844 and beyond.

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Both readings respect Rule IV (history confirms prophecy). Bohr's 1798 start is a Rule-V extension grounded in Rev 10:11 ("thou must prophesy again"), which Smith himself acknowledges as the same movement under a different chapter; the disagreement is on the **window's starting bracket**, not on its substance.

verses.text: Saying with a loud voice, Fear God, and give glory to him; for the hour of his judgment is come: and worship him that made heaven, and earth, and the sea, and the fountains of waters.

pioneerReadings.html: EGW: "_Fear God, and give glory to him; for the hour of his judgment is come_" is the **specific Millerite 1840-1844 cry** based on Dan 8:14: "This warning is brought to view in Revelation 14. Here is a threefold message represented as proclaimed by heavenly beings and immediately followed by the coming of the Son of man to reap 'the harvest of the earth.' The first of these warnings announces the approaching judgment" (GC 311.3). The hour-of-judgment is the **investigative judgment** beginning in heaven Oct 22, 1844 — "_Worship Him that made heaven and earth_" anticipates the Sabbath-test of the third angel (the Sabbath is the **creation memorial** identifying the true God).

Smith reads "**the hour of his judgment is come**" as the **investigative judgment beginning in 1844** at the close of the 2300-day prophecy. The full chronological argument runs from pp. 588-621 (the bulk of the entire chapter). The worship-the-Creator call Smith reads as the Sabbath call: "**the language of the first angel calls our attention to the fourth commandment in unmistakable terms: 'Worship him that made heaven, and earth, and the sea, and the fountains of waters'**" (DAR 621.1-2, with cross-reference to Exod 20:11).

Miller's calculation: the 2300 days of Dan 8:14 end in 1843/44, marking the **commencement of the judgment hour**. This is the warrant for the loud-voice cry: the hour of judgment is *now* (not future, not past); fear God and give Him glory because the end is at hand. Miller's view of the judgment is the **executive judgment at the Second Coming** (he did not have the post-1844 sanctuary correction) — but the cry itself is the same one Andrews and Smith inherit and refine.

Litch's Ottoman-trumpet prediction (the literal Aug 11, 1840 fall of Ottoman supremacy) is the **providential vindication** of the judgment-hour cry. After the 1840 fulfillment, "the advent proclamation comes in at the right time in this prophecy" (cited verbatim in TMR 28.2). For Litch the hour of judgment = the **final judgment scene** introduced by Christ's second coming.

James White (biographer of Bates) treats v. 7 as the **vindicated Millerite cry** anchored by Litch's 1840 Ottoman fulfillment: "_These astounding facts prove that the prophecy of the sounding of the sixth angel for three hundred and ninety-one years and fifteen days, ended on the 11th day of August, 1840, and at the same time the second woe passed, and behold, the third woe cometh quickly_" (LELJB 268.3). The judgment-hour cry follows immediately upon the second-woe terminus and is the public testimony that the **time of the end** has begun.

Andrews: the hour-of-judgment cry **could not have been preached in any past age** because the prophecies fixing the judgment-time (Dan 8:14, 12:4-9) were "_closed up and sealed till the time of the end_" (TMR 16.2). Therefore it is exclusively the **1840-1844 Millerite proclamation** that fulfills v. 7. Andrews quotes Bliss: "_As an indication of the approach of the end, there was, however, to be seen another angel flying through the midst of heaven, having the everlasting gospel to preach unto them that dwell on the earth... The burden of this angel was to be the same gospel which had been before proclaimed; but connected with it was the additional element..._" (TMR 27.3). The "worship the Creator" clause directly anticipates the **Sabbath issue** of vv. 9-12.

Crosier supplies the **sanctuary basis for the judgment-hour cry**: "_the atonement was made in the Sanctuary, but Calvary was not such a place... He did not begin the work of making the atonement... till after His ascension, when by His own blood He entered His heavenly Sanctuary for us_" (SANC 19.4-19.6). The 2300-day terminus (Oct 22, 1844) marks Christ's entry into the Most Holy Place to begin the **investigative phase** — this is what the first angel proclaims has begun: "_the hour of His judgment is come_," not is coming.

Haskell ties the judgment-hour cry to **the opening of the Most Holy Place ministry** Oct 22, 1844. The worship-the-Creator clause is the **doctrinal seed** of the third angel's Sabbath testing: the Sabbath = the creation memorial = the sign that distinguishes worshipers of the true God from worshipers of the beast.

Jones: the judgment-hour cry is paired with the **worship-the-Creator** test that defines true Protestantism against the apostasy: "_the Lord sends the everlasting gospel to all, calling them to worship Him alone, who made heaven, and earth, and the sea, and the fountains of waters, for the hour of His judgment is come. And the sign which He himself has set up that men may know that He is the Lord, the true God, who made..._" (ECE 872.6 → 873.1, sign = Sabbath). The whole three-angel sequence is **anti-apostate-Protestantism**.

Bates: the hour-of-judgment cry is the **1844 movement** preparing for the third angel's restoration of the Sabbath: the worship-the-Creator clause directly anticipates the fourth-commandment restoration. "_Why this third angel's loud cry about the commandments of God, because the fourth one, which had been trodden down for many generations, is to be restored_" (BP3 140.3) — and that restoration is the proper fulfillment of the worship-the-Creator clause.

Loughborough as historian-witness: the judgment-hour cry was the **defining Millerite proclamation** based on the 2300 days (Dan 8:14) terminating Oct 22, 1844. "_They believed, in harmony with Revelation 3:7, 8 and other scriptures, that at the close of the twenty-three hundred days of Daniel 8:14, Christ closed his work in the first apartment of the heavenly sanctuary, and changed his ministration to the most holy, and entered upon the work of the judgment_" (GSAM 226.3, testimony of 21 witnesses). The cry was vindicated by Christ's actual sanctuary movement, not invalidated by the Disappointment.

Fitch participates in the judgment-hour cry from 1841 until his death in October 1844 — he is one of the **leading preachers** of the imminent literal advent and the hour of judgment. His most famous contribution belongs to v. 8 (the 1843 'Come Out of Her, My People' sermon).

modernReadings.citation: _From the Close of Probation to the New Earth_, p. 66; _Studies on Daniel 11_, on Rev 14:7
modernReadings.html: Bohr reads "hour of judgment" as the 1844 investigative judgment and the worship-the-Creator call as a Sabbath call: "**the Creator is the Sabbath (Revelation 14:7)**" (_Studies on Daniel 11_, near line 8912). "**Revelation 14:7 calls us to worship the creator in contrast to worshipping the beast and his image (Revelation 14:9-11)**" (_Studies on Daniel 11_, near line 9030). The hour-of-judgment / Creator / Sabbath chain matches Smith's Rule-V argument exactly.

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Exemplary Rule-IV and Rule-V convergence (Lev 16 / Dan 7-8 / Exod 20:11 chain).

verses.text: And there followed another angel, saying, Babylon is fallen, is fallen, that great city, because she made all nations drink of the wine of the wrath of her fornication.

pioneerReadings.html: EGW defines Babylon as **apostate religion** in general: "The term 'Babylon' is derived from 'Babel,' and signifies confusion. It is employed in Scripture to designate the various forms of false or apostate religion" (GC 381.1). Babylon = Rome plus her **harlot daughters** (the fallen Protestant churches): "Babylon is said to be 'the mother of harlots.' By her daughters must be symbolized churches that cling to her doctrines and traditions, and follow her example of sacrificing the truth and the approval of God, in order to form an unlawful alliance with the world. The message of Revelation 14, announcing the fall of Babylon must apply to religious bodies that were once pure and have become corrupt" (GC 382.3). The fall is a **moral fall** caused by rejecting the advent message and embracing worldliness: "The second angel's message of Revelation 14 was first preached in the summer of 1844, and it then had a more direct application to the churches of the United States, where the warning of the judgment had been most widely proclaimed and most generally rejected" (GC 389.2). But "the message of the second angel did not reach its complete fulfillment in 1844" — the **Loud Cry of Rev 18:1-5** is its future climactic repeat (GC 389.2-390).

Smith reads the second angel as the **1844 cry** by the Millerite movement (with future Loud-Cry repeat acknowledged but treated as secondary): "**This proclamation was made in the summer of 1844, when about fifty thousand withdrew from the various nominal churches in this country... A second proclamation of this same message is brought to view in Revelation 18:1-4, where the fall of Babylon is again announced, with the addition that she had become 'the hold of every foul spirit and the cage of every unclean and hateful bird'**" (DAR 624.1-625.2, condensed). On Babylon = the apostate churches: "**The term Babylon, when applied to the religious world, must signify the apostate churches, embracing the great mother of harlots and her daughters**" (DAR 622.1, cross-linking Rev 17).

Miller treats Babylon primarily as **Roman Catholicism** (the harlot of Rev 17) — though by 1843 the Millerites broadened the term to include any apostate Protestant body that rejected the advent message. The fall (v. 8) is the **moral/spiritual fall** that follows the rejection of present truth, not the future destruction of vv. 19-20.

Litch identifies Babylon with **the papal/apostate religious system** and reads the fall as her loss of spiritual/moral standing (not loss of civil power) at the precise time the second angel's cry sounds (1843-44). The wine of her fornication = the **false doctrines** through which she has intoxicated the nations.

James White: the second angel's cry was first publicly preached by Charles Fitch in summer 1843, sounded throughout the Millerite movement in 1844, and was followed by literal separation from the nominal churches — a separation James White personally experienced and led. The fall is the **rejection by the Spirit** of churches that rejected the first angel's message.

Andrews' full second-angel exposition (TMR ch. 3): "_THE first important inquiry relates to the time when this proclamation is to be made. As this angel follows the one who proclaims the hour of God's Judgment, it is evident that this proclamation is the next event in order_" (TMR 36.2). Babylon = **the entire apostate religious world** — Catholic and Protestant. The fall is **not** the loss of civil power (TMR 50.3-50.7) and **not** her future destruction (TMR 50.1-50.2). "_We understand that the fall of Babylon is her rejection by God, that the Holy Spirit leaves her in consequence of her alienation from God and her union with the world, and that thus she is left to the spirits of devils_" (TMR 50.7). The proclamation was made "_in immediate connection with the proclamation of the hour of God's Judgment_" — i.e., from 1843-44 — and Andrews documents it through Himes, Miller's letters, and contemporary witnesses (TMR 55.1-65). **Critical Andrews position**: Babylon's wine = the **false doctrines** (immortality of soul, Sunday-keeping, etc.) by which she has corrupted the world; the call to "come out" is a **literal call to separate from fallen Protestantism**.

Haskell follows Andrews and EGW: Babylon = **apostate Christianity** (Rome + her Protestant daughters); the fall is the **moral fall in 1844** completed in the future Loud Cry of Rev 18:1-5. He emphasizes the **two-stage** structure (1844 partial, future complete) inherited from EGW (GC 389.2).

Jones reads v. 8 as the announcement of the **fall of apostate Protestantism** — the church bodies that have formed the Image of the Beast by uniting with the state. Babylon's fall is the necessary **prelude to the third angel's mark-of-beast warning**: she is fallen *first*, then her wickedness is exposed in the call to "come out" so that none should share her plagues.

Bates: "_The rushing from the sectional, sectarian branches of the nominal church (or Sardis state,) at that time, has no parallel. All, all was tending to a state of brotherly love_" (BP3 138.1). The fall of Babylon = the **Sardis-state churches** rejected at the second-angel's cry; the advent body comes out and forms the **Philadelphia-state** (the seventh-day Sabbath-keeping remnant) — "_The second angel's message and voice from heaven required God's people to leave the churches_" (BP3 140.3).

Loughborough records the historical fact: "_at that time [1844] the rushing from the sectional, sectarian branches of the nominal church... has no parallel_" (cf. Bates BP3 138.1). The second-angel cry produced literal **physical separation** of advent believers from their churches — a separation Loughborough himself witnessed and documented in many of the early Adventist biographies.

pioneerReadings.citation: Fitch, 'Come Out of Her, My People' (1843); LCFMC 1.1
pioneerReadings.html: Fitch is the **originating preacher** of the second-angel cry. His 1843 sermon 'Come Out of Her, My People' first publicly applied Rev 18:4 and Rev 14:8 to the **Protestant churches that rejected the advent message**, identifying them as Babylon along with Rome. "_Our dear Brother Storrs says that the Great Head of the Church designed that we should come out of Babylon, and not wait for Babylon to thrust us out. I believe he is right, and I am therefore determined to come out_" (LCFMC 1.1, Feb 1844). Fitch then publicly proclaimed himself "separated from" his ministerial association and called all advent believers to do the same. **This is the most significant Fitch contribution in the entire study series** — without his sermon the second-angel cry would not have launched.

modernReadings.citation: _From the Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 66, with extended treatment quoted from his Rev 14:8 + 18:1-5 comparison table
modernReadings.html: Bohr explicitly stages v. 8 as a **two-stage** message: "**The Millerites proclaimed the message of Revelation 14:8 leading up to 1844**" (Bohr, _Close of Probation_, citing GC 603-604 and GC 390 verbatim). The future Loud Cry of Rev 18:1-5 is treated as the **swelling repetition** of the second angel. Bohr's comparison table maps Rev 14:8 onto Rev 18:1-5 point for point. **Convergent with extension**: Smith acknowledges the Loud-Cry repetition but treats the 1844 cry as primary; Bohr treats both as one composite message in two stages.

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Both readings respect Rule IV (history) and Rule V (Rev 18:1-5 as the prophetic parallel). The difference is one of emphasis.

symbols.label: Symbols redefined
symbols.html: Babylon (both: apostate religious world — Rome plus apostate Protestantism — per Rev 17).

verses.text: And the third angel followed them, saying with a loud voice, If any man worship the beast and his image, and receive his mark in his forehead, or in his hand,

pioneerReadings.html: EGW: the third angel begins his cry **when Christ entered the Most Holy Place** (Oct 22, 1844). "_As the ministration of Jesus closed in the holy place, and He passed into the holiest, and stood before the ark containing the law of God, He sent another mighty angel with a third message to the world. A parchment was placed in the angel's hand, and as he descended to the earth in power and majesty, he proclaimed a fearful warning, with the most terrible threatening ever borne to man_" (EW 254.1). The third angel's warning is bound to the **opening of the Most Holy Place** and the discovery of the fourth commandment shining brighter than the others (EW 255.1). "_The Real Issue in the Final Conflict—[Revelation 14:9, 10 quoted.] It is for the interest of all to understand what the mark of the beast is, and how they may escape the dread threatenings of God_" (7BC 979.9). Beast = Roman Papacy; Image of the Beast = apostate Protestantism in alliance with civil power; Mark = **Sunday observance** as token of papal authority over divine law.

Smith reads the third angel as the **Sabbath/Sunday warning** against the papal beast (Rev 13:1-10) and the Protestant-American image (Rev 13:11-17). The beast = papacy; the image = the American Protestant religious-civil coalition; the mark = Sunday observance. Smith treats v. 9 as the message header; the substantive identification runs through vv. 9-12.

Litch on the third angel's plague-and-vial fulfillment: "_What a terrible scene! with all their grievous sores, blood to drink—stagnant blood—and putrid fish filling the waters, scorched with burning heat; and then, to crown the whole, the kingdom of Anti-christ is to be full of darkness_" (Litch in TMR 119.2). The warning of v. 9 introduces the **plague-execution** of vv. 10-11 against those who refuse to come out from the beast.

James White: the third angel's warning is sounded from 1844 onward, growing in volume as the Sabbath-keeping remnant is gathered. The warning prepares God's people for the **future enforcement of the mark** by the two-horned beast (USA). The beast and image and mark are an inseparable triad.

Andrews' **canonical** third-angel exposition (TMR ch. 5): "_THIS is the most solemn warning that the Bible contains_" (TMR 73.2). The beast = the papacy (Rev 13:1-10); the image = formed by the two-horned beast (USA, Rev 13:11ff); the mark = the institution by which the papacy assumes prerogative over divine law. "_The work of the two-horned beast in performing miracles, and causing those that dwell upon the earth, by means of those miracles, to form an image to the beast which all men must worship, certainly pertains to the future. The Third Angel's Message is a warning to prepare the church for this fearful scene_" (TMR 74.2). On the mark: "_It is the mark of that beast to whom the image was made—the first beast... it is enforced by the two-horned beast; hence we understand it is an institution of the papacy, enforced by Protestantism_" (TMR 106.2). Andrews then identifies the mark with **Sunday observance**: the papacy's most distinctive claim is to have changed the day of worship (TMR 107.1-108.3). The third angel's cry is **the present truth** for the post-1844 remnant.

Haskell ties the third angel to the **opening of the Most Holy Place** Oct 22, 1844, when the law of God was discovered with the Sabbath unaltered. The warning is **the most fearful in Scripture** because it pertains to **the final test** of allegiance — beast/image/mark versus commandments-of-God/faith-of-Jesus. The beast = Papacy; image = apostate-Protestant state-church to be formed in America (Rev 13:11ff); mark = **Sunday observance enforced by civil law**.

Jones positions the third angel as the **final counter-protest** against the apostate-Protestant Image of the Beast: "_just here while all are to be compelled to worship the papacy and its image, and to receive its mark, the Lord sends the everlasting gospel to all, calling them to worship Him alone... for the hour of His judgment is come_" (ECE 872.6). The mark is enforced by the **church-state union** in the USA. Jones's distinctive contribution: framing the third angel as a question of **religious liberty** and the rejection of **all** church-state coercion.

Bates' framing: "_The third angel in Revelation 14:9-13, continues his message and cry, until Jesus leaves the temple and is seated on the great white cloud. He begins as soon as the 2300 days end, and the door in the temple opens, for here is where he gets his message_" (BP3 139.2). The third angel's message comes from the **opened Most Holy Place** (the ark with the unaltered law); the warning against the mark is correlative with the **restoration of the Sabbath**: "_Why this third angel's loud cry about the commandments of God, because the fourth one, which had been trodden down for many generations, is to be restored_" (BP3 140.3). **Bates is the first to systematically frame the Sabbath as seal of God = identifier vs. the mark of the beast.**

Loughborough (in his treatise on the two-horned beast, the same one cited on Rev 13:11ff) frames the third angel as the warning against **the future Sunday-law crisis** brought about by the United States acting as the lamb-like beast. The mark is the **public allegiance-token of papal authority** — Sunday-keeping enforced by civil power. The third angel is the **present truth** for the Sabbath-keeping remnant.

Fitch's 1843 'Come Out of Her, My People' sermon (the second-angel cry) implicitly carries the third-angel logic forward: those who refuse to leave the apostate churches receive the warning of vv. 9-11. Fitch died Oct 14, 1844 before the post-Disappointment sanctuary correction, so he did not develop the mature mark-of-beast=Sunday identification.

modernReadings.citation: _From the Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 372-378
modernReadings.html: Bohr reads the third angel as the warning against the Rev 13 beast (papacy) and its image (apostate Protestant America in coalition with Rome). Verbatim on the message's character: "**the third angel's message contains the message of righteousness by faith but rather it is righteousness by faith**" (p. 374). The mark is Sunday observance enforced by the image. **Convergent on every substantive identification** with Smith.

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Exemplary Rule-IV / Rule-V convergence.

verses.text: The same shall drink of the wine of the wrath of God, which is poured out without mixture into the cup of his indignation; and he shall be tormented with fire and brimstone in the presence of the holy angels, and in the presence of the Lamb:

pioneerReadings.html: EGW: "_drink of the wine of the wrath of God_" = the **seven last plagues** (Rev 16) poured out unmixed-with-mercy upon those who receive the mark. The fire-and-brimstone scene is the **final destruction in the lake of fire** at the close of the millennium — not eternal-conscious-torment in the Catholic sense, but the final annihilation. "_Coal and Oil Agencies in Final Destruction—Those majestic trees which God had caused to grow upon the earth, for the benefit of the inhabitants of the old world, and which they had used to form into idols... God has reserved in the earth, in the form of coal and oil, to use Himself in their final destruction_" (1BC 1090.8). The torment "_in the presence of the Lamb_" is the final-judgment scene where every knee bows.

Smith reads "wine of the wrath of God without mixture" as the seven last plagues of Rev 15-16: "**The seven last plagues are the wrath of God without mixture, and these are sent upon those who worship the beast and his image, and receive his mark. These are the unmingled judgments of God, unmixed with mercy**" (DAR 631.2, cross-linking Rev 15:1 + 16:1-2). The fire-and-brimstone torment is the executive judgment.

Litch (quoted by Andrews): "_What a terrible scene! with all their grievous sores, blood to drink—stagnant blood—and putrid fish filling the waters, scorched with burning heat; and then, to crown the whole, the kingdom of Anti-christ is to be full of darkness. Oh, what a picture of woe!_" (TMR 119.2). The wine of wrath = the **literal plague execution** upon mark-receivers.

James White: the wine of wrath is the **literal plague execution** beginning when probation closes; the torment is in the presence of the Lamb because Christ is still mediating until probation ends, then comes out from the sanctuary and the plagues fall.

Andrews ties v. 10 directly to the **seven last plagues** of Rev 16: "_The wine of the wrath of God unmixed with mercy must be far more dreadful than the judgment inflicted on Egypt_" (TMR 117.3). He systematically maps each plague to v. 10's cup-of-indignation language, with the seven vials as the **execution** of the threat the third angel utters. The torment is the **literal plague-execution** (boils, blood, scorching, darkness, hail, blood-bath of Armageddon, the great earthquake).

Haskell follows the SDA consensus: wine of wrath = **seven last plagues + final fire of Rev 20**. The torment is **finite annihilation** (consistent with the SDA conditional-immortality teaching established by Storrs and Bates) — not endless conscious torment.

Jones positions v. 10 as the consequence of the apostate-Protestant church's success in restoring papal principles: "_This brings about the situation described in Revelation 13:8. And this in turn develops the fulfillment of Revelation 18:8_" (ECE 873.1) — the plague-fire that consumes Babylon and her worshipers.

Bates: the wrath cup is poured out in the **seven last plagues** that fall after probation closes — "_A great voice from the temple directs them to go and pour out the vials of wrath, and when the seventh is poured out, a great voice from the temple, from the throne, saying, it is done... This is the voice of God_" (BP3 139.1). The third angel's continuing cry runs from 1844 until probation closes and the plagues begin.

Fitch on the literal fire-destruction of the wicked: "_Thus I have found in every one of the prophets except Jonah, an express declaration respecting the destruction of the wicked from off the earth. Nearly all the prophets speak of fire as the agent of that destruction_" (LJL 23.1). The fire-and-brimstone of v. 10 is the **literal final destruction**, not metaphor for endless conscious torment.

modernReadings.citation: _From the Close of Probation to the New Earth_, p. 374; _Revelation's Seven Trumpets_, pp. 20-21
modernReadings.html: Bohr reads v. 10 against the **seven plagues** backdrop and ties it to the altar-fire angel of Rev 14:18 / Rev 8:5 / Rev 15-16: "**fire who pours out God's wrath into the winepress of His fury (Revelation 14:18)**" (_Seven Trumpets_, pp. 20-21). The wine-without-mixture chain runs Rev 14:10 → Rev 15:1 → Rev 16:1-2 → Rev 19:11ff. **Convergent with Smith on the wrath-without-mixture = seven plagues chain.**

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Convergent; Rule V chain (Rev 14 → 15 → 16 → 19).

verses.text: And the smoke of their torment ascendeth up for ever and ever: and they have no rest day nor night, who worship the beast and his image, and whosoever receiveth the mark of his name.

pioneerReadings.html: EGW reads "_the smoke of their torment ascendeth up for ever and ever_" as **the eternal effect of a finite punishment** — not eternal conscious torment but **eternal annihilation** whose smoke (= memorial of judgment) endures. The wicked are **completely consumed** in the final fire; the "no rest day nor night" refers to the duration of the punishment in time, not eternity (cf. Isa 34:9-10's identical language about Edom's literal cities now in ruins). **Convergent SDA reading: conditional immortality.**

Smith handles the "for ever and ever" smoke as the **final and complete destruction** (annihilation, not eternal conscious torment), citing Isa 34 (Edom's smoke ascending forever) and Jude 7 (Sodom and Gomorrah set forth as an example) as the Rule-V parallel: the smoke ascending "for ever and ever" describes the **utter and irreversible** character of the destruction, not endless duration of conscious suffering. "**It is here, in the lake of fire, that the wicked will receive the full reward of their works; for the smoke of their torment ascendeth up forever and ever — that is, while they have being, or until they are utterly consumed**" (DAR 632.2-633.1, with Edom/Sodom typology).

Andrews: the smoke and torment language is **plague-execution language**, not eternal-conscious-torment language. The mark-receivers experience the plague-process and final fire in real time; the smoke is **proportionate to their guilt**, ascending until their utter destruction. Andrews follows the early-Adventist conditional-immortality teaching (Storrs, Bates) — distinct from the traditional Protestant reading.

Haskell follows the SDA conditional-immortality teaching: smoke ascending forever = **the eternal effect of the finite fire**, not endless conscious torment. The wicked are utterly consumed (Mal 4:1, Ps 37:10).

Bates' Sardis-state framework: those who refuse to leave the apostate churches share in their plague-execution; the smoke and no-rest language is the literal plague-process before final annihilation, not eternal conscious torment.

Fitch was a leading **conditional-immortality** advocate in the 1840s movement (with George Storrs); the smoke-ascending language is the eternal effect of the literal fire of final destruction (cf. LJL 23). The wicked **cease to exist** rather than suffer endless conscious torment.

modernReadings.html: (Bohr does not give v. 11 a separate verse-by-verse treatment; the substance is covered under the three-angels block at _Close of Probation_, p. 66, and the executive-judgment block at pp. 372-378, with annihilationism implicit in his treatment of the lake of fire in the New Earth volume.)

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No discrete Bohr exegesis to score; Smith's annihilationist reading is the SDA-standard Rule-V (Isa 34 / Jude 7 / Mal 4:1-3 / 2 Pet 2:6) reading.

verses.text: Here is the patience of the saints: here are they that keep the commandments of God, and the faith of Jesus.

pioneerReadings.html: EGW: "_Here is the patience of the saints: here are they that keep the commandments of God, and the faith of Jesus_" is **the SDA identity verse**: "_The third angel closes his message thus: 'Here is the patience of the saints: here are they that keep the commandments of God, and the faith of Jesus.' As he repeated these words, he pointed to the heavenly sanctuary. The minds of all who embrace this message are directed to the most holy place, where Jesus stands before the ark, making His final intercession_" (EW 254.1). The **commandments of God** include the Sabbath (the 4th commandment shining brighter than the others, EW 255.1); the **faith of Jesus** = both the gospel-faith Jesus had and the faith that has Jesus as its object — i.e., righteousness-by-faith. The two together constitute the **complete SDA platform**.

Smith reads the verse as the **distinguishing badge** of the third-angel people: commandment-keeping (specifically the fourth commandment, against the mark) and the faith of Jesus. "**Here we have the very people brought to view who keep the commandments of God, including, of course, the fourth, requiring the observance of the seventh-day Sabbath, in the face of the message of the third angel which prohibits the worship of the beast or his image, or the receiving of his mark**" (DAR 633.1).

Litch: the patience of the saints clause is the **present-tense identifier** of the third-angel company — those who, while the warning is being given, persevere in keeping the commandments and the faith of Jesus despite the threat of the beast and his image. The faith of Jesus = the saving gospel of righteousness imputed.

James White: v. 12 is the SDA platform-statement — the same one he and Ellen and Bates built upon from 1845 onward. **Commandments of God** = the unchanged Ten (including the Sabbath); **faith of Jesus** = the saving gospel of Christ's righteousness imputed to the believer.

Andrews: "_The language of verse 12, 'Here is the patience of the saints,' is sufficient of itself to overthrow the application of these messages to the future age_" (TMR 13.1) — i.e., this is the present-truth identifier of the remnant during probation. The commandments include the Sabbath, which the papacy claims to have changed: "_By the observance of the Sabbath, men acknowledge the great Creator as their God; but when they understandingly choose in its stead the day enforced by the man of sin, they acknowledge the supremacy of the apostate power_" (TMR 108.3). The two clauses define **SDA distinctive identity**: keeping the Sabbath under persecution + holding Jesus as the sole object of saving faith.

Haskell: v. 12 is the **culminating identifier** of the three-angel remnant. The commandments of God (Sabbath included) plus the faith of Jesus (righteousness-by-faith) form the **two-sided test**: the legalist who keeps the law without Christ's righteousness fails; the antinomian who professes faith without obedience fails. Only the **commandments-plus-faith** company endures.

Jones (with Waggoner): the **faith of Jesus** is the **same gospel of righteousness** that the apostate Protestants have lost; the commandments of God are kept by faith in Jesus, not by works. The third angel's message is therefore both **doctrinal** (Sabbath + commandments) and **soteriological** (justification by faith). The two are inseparable.

Waggoner's **canonical contribution**: the "faith of Jesus" of v. 12 = **righteousness by faith** — the gospel rediscovered at 1888 and proclaimed jointly with Jones. The commandments cannot be kept apart from the faith of Jesus; the faith of Jesus produces the keeping of the commandments. Waggoner frames v. 12 as the **proof-text** that the third angel's message and righteousness-by-faith are inseparable — a critique of any reading of v. 12 that emphasizes commandments-keeping alone.

Bates: "_The third angel in Revelation 14:9-13, continues his message and cry... Hear him—'Here is the patience of the saints, here are they who keep the commandments of God, and the faith of Jesus.' Why cry this? Because he sees the unaltered Covenant of God in the opened temple_" (BP3 139.2). The commandments-keeping (esp. Sabbath) is the necessary remnant identifier; the faith of Jesus is faith in the High Priest now in the Most Holy Place.

Loughborough: v. 12 is the **doctrinal seal** of the third-angel remnant — those who keep the seventh-day Sabbath under the future Sunday-law crisis while holding fast to the faith of Jesus. The two clauses jointly constitute the **mark of the saved** opposed to the mark of the beast.

modernReadings.citation: _From the Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 372-378
modernReadings.html: Bohr reads v. 12 as the third-angel saints — characterized by commandment-keeping plus the faith of Jesus (righteousness by faith): "**the third angel's message... is righteousness by faith**" (p. 374). Bohr ties the v. 12 character profile to the Rev 14:1-5 character portrait. **Convergent with Smith.**

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Convergent.

verses.text: And I heard a voice from heaven saying unto me, Write, Blessed are the dead which die in the Lord from henceforth: Yea, saith the Spirit, that they may rest from their labours; and their works do follow them.

pioneerReadings.html: EGW: "_Blessed are the dead which die in the Lord from henceforth_" — the **henceforth = from 1844 onward**, after the third angel's message has begun. Those who die in the Lord after this point are blessed because (a) they sleep through the time of trouble such as never was and (b) they come up at the special resurrection of Dan 12:2 to behold the Second Coming of Christ. The works that follow them = the testimony their lives have borne to the three angels' messages.

pioneerReadings.citation: DAR 633.2-635.1, with footnote on the special resurrection
pioneerReadings.html: Smith reads "**from henceforth**" as **from the rise of the third angel's message** — i.e., from 1844 onward. The blessing is on those who die in the faith of the third angel: "**This blessing must apply to a definite class who die in a definite time; otherwise the announcement would have no special significance. The class who die in the Lord from henceforth, must mean those who die in the faith of the third angel's message; and the time must be from the rise of that message, which dates from 1844**" (DAR 633.2-634.1). Smith adds a footnote on the **special resurrection** of Dan 12:2 — a partial resurrection just prior to the Second Coming, raising those who died in the faith of the third angel since 1844 plus those who pierced Christ (Rev 1:7).

Litch: v. 13 is a **probationary-period text** (saints can still die in the Lord, therefore the messages are still being preached). The henceforth marks the beginning of the third angel's message; the works that follow = the testimony of the obedient life.

James White: v. 13 was a comfort verse in the early Adventist movement after the Disappointment — believers who had died with the Advent hope (including their own children) were declared blessed because they sleep through the time of trouble. The verse vindicates the Sabbath-keeping remnant against the charge that the Disappointment proved their faith vain.

Andrews uses v. 13 as **a chronological anchor**: "_verse 13, which pronounces a blessing on the dead which die in the Lord from henceforth, that is, from a point of time as late, at least, as the third Angel's Message, presents a testimony which cannot be evaded. It demonstrates that this part of John's vision relates to a period prior to the first resurrection; for the saints cannot die after being made immortal. 1 Corinthians 15:51-56_" (TMR 14.1). The henceforth = **from the giving of the third message onward** (post-1844). This proves the three messages cannot be located in the future age.

Haskell: the henceforth-blessing of v. 13 distinguishes those who **die under the third angel's message** (post-1844) — these come up in a special resurrection (Dan 12:2) to witness Christ's return. The verse is **proof** that the three messages belong to the present era, not the future.

Bates: v. 13 belongs to the **same third-angel proclamation block** (vv. 9-13 read as a unit) — "_The third angel in Revelation 14:9-13, continues his message and cry, until Jesus leaves the temple_" (BP3 139.2). The henceforth = from 1844 onward; the blessing covers the entire third-angel era including the time-of-trouble dead.

Loughborough: v. 13 is the **vindication-verse for the Adventist dead** who died after 1844 in the third-angel message — "_They believed that those who had the clear light upon the first angel's message, and turned against it, bitterly opposing it, were rejected of God_" (GSAM 226.4) — but those who died in the message are blessed and will come up in the special resurrection. Personal historical importance for the early SDA community whose pioneers were dying.

Fitch's life is a **personal fulfillment** of v. 13: he died on Oct 14, 1844 just days before the Oct 22 Disappointment, the first major Millerite preacher to die in the Lord under the just-begun three-angels' messages. His death (from exhaustion after baptizing converts in cold water) is regarded by SDA tradition as the inaugural application of the henceforth-blessing.

modernReadings.citation: _From the Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 221-227
modernReadings.html: Bohr makes the special resurrection the centerpiece of his v. 13 treatment, anchoring it to Dan 12:2: "**Therefore, God pronounces a special blessing upon those who die in the faith of the third angel's message from 1844 on**" (p. 221, condensed; full treatment pp. 221-227). Bohr links Rev 14:13 → Dan 12:2 → Rev 1:7 in the same chain Smith uses. **Convergent on substance**; Bohr expands the special-resurrection treatment Smith places in a footnote into a 6-page chapter exposition.

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Exemplary Rule-V convergence (Rev 14:13 + Dan 12:2 + Rev 1:7).

verses.text: And I looked, and behold a white cloud, and upon the cloud one sat like unto the Son of man, having on his head a golden crown, and in his hand a sharp sickle.

pioneerReadings.html: EGW identifies the Son-of-man on the white cloud as **Christ at the Second Coming**: "_The thick cloud appears in the east. It is the cloud which surrounds the Saviour and which at a distance seems shrouded in darkness. The people of God know this to be the sign of the Son of man. In solemn silence they gaze upon it as it draws nearer the earth, becoming lighter and more glorious, until it is a great white cloud_" (paraphrasing GC 640-641). The golden crown = His kingship in the kingdom now being established; the sharp sickle = the executive instrument of the **harvest of the righteous** (vv. 15-16).

Smith identifies the Son of man on the cloud as **Christ at the Second Coming**, with the harvest of vv. 15-16 as the **ingathering of the righteous**: "**The Son of man is Christ. He comes upon a white cloud, having on his head a golden crown, and in his hand a sharp sickle. The harvest is the end of the world (Matthew 13:39); and the reapers are the angels**" (DAR 634.2, cross-linking Matt 13:30, 39, 41).

Miller: the Son-of-man scene = **the literal personal return of Christ** in the clouds (Acts 1:11; Matt 24:30). This was Miller's central distinctive against the postmillennial spiritualizers of his day — the harvest is *not* a gradual gospel ingathering but Christ's **physical reaping** at His Second Coming.

Litch: the Son-of-man on the cloud is **Christ's personal advent**, the same scene as Dan 7:13-14 (one like the Son of man brought near to the Ancient of Days, here coming forth with the kingdom received). The white cloud is the chariot of His descent.
James White: the Son-of-man scene = **the Second Coming** following the close of Christ's High-Priestly ministry (SATDSD). The crown and sickle indicate the **transition from Mediator to King**.

Andrews: the Son-of-man on the cloud = **Christ at the Second Advent** (the same scene as Matt 24:30; 1 Thess 4:16). The harvest of the earth (v. 15) is the **ingathering of the righteous saints** at the Second Coming. The sharp sickle = the instrument of harvest separation. Convergent with Smith.

Haskell: the white cloud + golden crown + sharp sickle = **Christ at the Second Coming as King**, no longer Mediator in the sanctuary but Reaper of the righteous harvest. The scene immediately follows the close of probation.

Jones: the Son-of-man on the cloud follows immediately upon the **success of Leo's scheme** (papal church-state restoration via apostate Protestantism); the crown indicates the kingdom Christ now takes from the world-powers He destroys at His coming.

Bates: the Son-of-man on the cloud is the **immediate sequel** to the third angel's complete cry — "_the third angel... continues his message and cry, until Jesus leaves the temple and is seated on the great white cloud_" (BP3 139.2). The seating on the cloud marks the **end of the High-Priestly ministry** and the beginning of the harvest.

Loughborough: the Son-of-man on the cloud = **Christ's literal personal advent**, the same event the Millerites preached from 1840-1844 (though without the sanctuary-correction the Sabbatarian Adventists later added). The crown and sickle mark Christ as **King-Reaper** at the consummation.

Fitch's distinguishing emphasis was the **literal personal premillennial advent** of Christ — "_the two resurrections... will take place literally, as there laid down_" (LJL 14.2). The Son-of-man on the cloud is the same literal advent for which Fitch labored 1841-44.

modernReadings.citation: _From the Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 66-70, 194
modernReadings.html: Bohr identifies the Son of man as Christ at the Second Coming. Verbatim: "**Revelation 14:14-17: When the harvest of the earth is ripe, Jesus sits on a cloud and reaps it**" (_Close of Probation_, lines 5749-5836). On the literary structure: "**The message of the three angels ripens the harvest and the grapes for the harvest**" (p. 70). **Convergent with Smith on Son-of-man = Christ at the Second Coming.**

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Convergent.

verses.text: And another angel came out of the temple, crying with a loud voice to him that sat on the cloud, Thrust in thy sickle, and reap: for the time is come for thee to reap; for the harvest of the earth is ripe.

pioneerReadings.html: EGW: the angel from the temple crying "_thrust in thy sickle and reap_" represents the **finality of the close of probation** — the harvest of the earth is ripe = the righteous have come to full maturity under the latter rain, and now must be reaped before the plagues fall. The latter-rain ripening is described in GC 611.2: "_as the 'former rain' was given... at the opening of the gospel... so the 'latter rain' will be given at its close for the ripening of the harvest_."

Smith reads the angel from the temple as bearing the **close-of-probation announcement** to Christ — the harvest is ripe, the work of mediation is finished: "**Another angel issues from the temple, crying to him that sits on the cloud to thrust in his sickle and reap. He thus brings to Christ the announcement from the Father that the hour has come for the reaping work to be done; the harvest of the earth is ripe**" (DAR 635.1).

Miller: the harvest-of-the-earth language = **the literal end-of-the-world harvest** of Matt 13:39 ("_the harvest is the end of the world_"). This was Miller's defining text-cluster against the spiritualizers — the harvest is decisive, instantaneous, and brought by Christ's personal advent.
Litch: the harvest-call angel = the **executive signal** of the end-of-the-world reaping; the ripeness of the harvest follows immediately after the plagues (TMR 119.2 sequence).

James White: the temple-angel commanding the harvest = the **divine signal that probation has closed** and the High-Priestly ministry in the heavenly sanctuary is finished. The harvest follows immediately from the close of Christ's mediatorial work (cf. SATDSD on Christ leaving the sanctuary).

Andrews: the angel commanding the harvest = a **divine messenger** announcing the close of probation. The earth is **ripe** = the righteous are fully prepared by the latter rain to be translated; the wicked are fully ripe in iniquity to be cast into the winepress (vv. 18-19).

Haskell: the temple-angel's command = the **probation-closing signal** from the heavenly sanctuary. The harvest-ripeness language matches Joel 3:13 (sickle + harvest is ripe + winepress + grapes are ripe) — the close of probation precipitates the harvest of both vine and grain.

modernReadings.citation: _From the Close of Probation to the New Earth_, p. 194
modernReadings.html: "**Jesus harvests the righteous and they are gathered in spiritual Jerusalem (14:15, 16)**" (p. 194). The angel from the temple announces the harvest's ripeness. **Convergent with Smith.**

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Convergent.

verses.text: And he that sat on the cloud thrust in his sickle on the earth; and the earth was reaped.

pioneerReadings.html: EGW: the earth-reaping = **the gathering of the righteous to Christ at the Second Coming**: "_Soon there appears in the east a small black cloud, about half the size of a man's hand. It is the cloud which surrounds the Saviour and which seems in the distance to be shrouded in darkness. The people of God know this to be the sign of the Son of man. In solemn silence they gaze upon it..._" (GC 640.5-641.2). The reaping is the **resurrection of the righteous dead** + **translation of the living saints** (1 Thess 4:16-17).

"**The Saviour thrusts in his sickle, and the harvest of the earth is reaped. This is the gathering of the righteous, the wheat into the heavenly garner, at the second coming of Christ**" (DAR 635.2, cross-linking Matt 13:30).
Miller: the reaping = **the literal end of the world** when Christ comes in person, gathering His elect. This is the great event Miller spent his ministry preaching.
Litch: the reaping = the **literal personal second advent**, parallel to Matt 24:30-31, Dan 7:13-14. The earth is reaped = every righteous person is gathered to Christ.

James White: the actual reaping = the **literal Second Coming complex** (translation of living + resurrection of dead in Christ), the same event SATDSD positions immediately after the close of probation.

Andrews: the actual reaping = **the Second Coming and translation of the living saints + resurrection of the righteous dead** (1 Thess 4:16-17). "_The saints cannot die after being made immortal_" (TMR 14.1) — therefore the harvest immediately precedes the immortalization.

Haskell: the reaping is the **literal Second Coming** ingathering of the righteous; the earth is reaped = every righteous person (living and sleeping) is translated/resurrected and caught up to Christ.

modernReadings.citation: _From the Close of Probation to the New Earth_, p. 194
modernReadings.html: "**Jesus harvests the righteous and they are gathered in spiritual Jerusalem (14:15, 16)**" (p. 194). **Convergent with Smith on harvest = ingathering of the righteous at the Second Coming.**

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Convergent.

verses.text: And another angel came out of the temple which is in heaven, he also having a sharp sickle.

pioneerReadings.html: EGW: the second sickle-bearing angel = the **executive agent of judgment** sent against the wicked. The two harvests (grain v. 15-16; vintage vv. 17-20) are **two distinct ingatherings**: first the righteous reaped to Christ, then the wicked gathered for destruction. Convergent with the broader SDA reading.
Smith reads the **second angel** (with a second sickle) as a new symbol introducing the **vintage** — distinct from the harvest of vv. 14-16. The sickle is for the wicked.

Miller: the second angel-with-sickle = **the executive of the wicked harvest** at the literal end-of-the-world (Matt 13:39-42). The grain (v. 16) and the vine (v. 19) are reaped at the **same event**: the Second Coming.
Litch: the second sickle-angel = the **executive of the vintage** against the wicked. Litch treats grain and vintage harvests as parallel events at the Second Coming.

Andrews: this second angel + sickle = **the executive vintage of the wicked** at Armageddon (Rev 16:14-16, 19:11-21). "_The coming of Christ as a thief (see Matthew 24:42-44) does not take place until after the sixth vial is poured out. The gathering of the nations to the great battle, which is accomplished by the spirits, is brought to view in many scriptures. Joel 3:1, 2, 9-16; Zephaniah 3:8; Revelation 19:19-21_" (TMR 121.1). The temple-angel-with-sickle represents the **divine agency** of the destruction; the wicked are not destroyed by the saints' violence but by direct divine judgment.

Haskell: the second sickle-angel = the **executive of the vintage**; the wicked harvest (vv. 17-20) is a distinct event from the grain harvest (vv. 15-16) — though both occur at the close of probation and the Second Coming complex.

modernReadings.citation: _From the Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 70, 194; _Revelation's Seven Trumpets_, pp. 20-21
modernReadings.html: Bohr reads this angel as the executor of the vintage and links the altar-fire imagery: "**Revelation 14:14-17: When the harvest of the earth is ripe, Jesus sits on a cloud and reaps it**" (_Close of Probation_, p. 70, on the literary flow). On the second angel/sickle distinction: "**Revelation 14:17-20: When probation closes, God's spiritual Israel (the harvest) is gathered inside worldwide spiritual Mt. Zion and the wicked spiritual Babylonians (the grapes) are gathered in the worldwide spiritual winepress outside the city**" (_Close of Probation_, p. 70). **Convergent on the wicked-vintage identification.**

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Convergent.

verses.text: And another angel came out from the altar, which had power over fire; and cried with a loud cry to him that had the sharp sickle, saying, Thrust in thy sickle, and gather the clusters of the vine of the earth; for her grapes are fully ripe.

pioneerReadings.html: EGW: the angel-from-the-altar (the altar of incense, source of accepted prayers and unanswered cries for justice from the martyrs of Rev 6:9-10) directs the harvest of the **fully-ripe wicked**. Their cup of iniquity is full; the saints' prayers for vindication are now answered.
